title: TPU v1 versus v2+ · When to Move from Monolithic Silicon to 2.5D / Chiplets
oneLine: Packaging architecture decides whether to build one die or combine several. TPU v1 is monolithic; v2+ co-packages a logic die and HBM on a 2.5D interposer, while pods connect devices through ICI—an evolution driven by yield, memory bandwidth, and reticle limits.

legend.name: Monolithic v1 die
legend.desc: TPU v1 integrates the MXU, accumulators, and 24 MiB Unified Buffer on one 28 nm die no larger than 331 mm². One die is simple and avoids in-package links, but yield declines as area grows.

legend.name: Off-chip DDR3 in v1
legend.desc: v1 uses 8 GiB of board-level DDR3 at only ~30 GB/s. Package and PCB I/O limit bandwidth, motivating later placement of memory beside logic on an interposer.

legend.name: Logic die in v2+
legend.desc: Later generations separate compute into its own die, which can be fabricated independently in a suitable process. A smaller die offers better yield and technology flexibility.

legend.name: HBM over 2.5D in v2+
legend.desc: HBM replaces board-level DDR3 and connects to logic through a very wide, short interposer link. This delivers an order-of-magnitude bandwidth increase and directly addresses v1's feed bottleneck.

legend.name: Three triggers for multiple dies
legend.desc: Consider 2.5D or chiplets when any ceiling is reached: yield becomes unacceptable at large die area, board-level memory cannot feed compute, or the design exceeds reticle size.

steps.cap: ① Starting point—v1: integrate the MXU, accumulators, and 24 MiB Unified Buffer on one monolithic die.
② Expose the limit: off-chip DDR3 provides only ~30 GB/s over board wiring, so memory bandwidth saturates before growing compute.
③ Split in v2+: fabricate logic and HBM separately and co-package them on a silicon interposer, using a short, wide link for order-of-magnitude bandwidth.
④ Scale farther: connect multiple packaged devices horizontally with ICI to form a pod beyond one package's limits.
⑤ Decision rule: hitting a ceiling in yield, memory bandwidth, or reticle area signals the move to 2.5D or chiplets.

before: Make the packaging fork explicit: Build a monolithic die or partition functions across dies and combine them with 2.5D/chiplet packaging? This architecture decision constrains every later physical implementation step.

Decisions to make: Target die area; partitioning of logic, memory, and I/O; silicon interposer versus lower-cost organic substrate; board DDR versus co-packaged HBM; and provision for ICI-like scale-out.
Required inputs: Compute scale (§3.3), memory-bandwidth demand (§3.4), process yield curves and reticle area, HBM/interposer cost and supply, and package power and cooling (§3.9).
Hard constraints: Yield drops rapidly with die area; one exposure cannot exceed the reticle; board-level DDR has bandwidth and pin ceilings; and advanced packaging adds cost and test complexity.
during: Split only when necessary: v1 chooses one die because it avoids in-package link overhead and simplifies design and test. Monolithic is best while area, yield, and bandwidth remain within budget.

Let yield constrain area: At fixed defect density, larger dies have a higher probability of fatal defects. When yield becomes unacceptable, divide the design into smaller dies that yield better and can be discarded individually.

Let bandwidth motivate 2.5D: v1's ~30 GB/s DDR3 cannot feed a larger array. HBM placed beside logic on a silicon interposer uses a very wide, short interface for an order-of-magnitude increase—the key v2+ upgrade.
Respect the reticle wall: Lithography imposes a maximum exposure area. When more compute no longer fits on one die, partitioning or chiplets are the only ways around the physical limit.

Account for partitioning cost: Multiple dies improve yield and bandwidth but add interposer/HBM cost, link power and latency, and known-good-die test complexity. Optimize total system cost; do not partition reflexively.
Scale horizontally beyond a package: When one package also reaches its limit, connect multiple chips with ICI into a pod. Partitioning then moves from within the package to between packages.

result.label: v2+ memory packaging
result.value: HBM on a 2.5D silicon interposer; much higher bandwidth

after: Validate packaging feasibility: Model yield and cost, then evaluate signal integrity, link power, and thermals for 2.5D. Finalize only when both bandwidth and yield targets are credible.

Propagate constraints: Give physical implementation die boundaries, interposer and bump layout, package dimensions, and thermal requirements; reconcile DDR versus co-packaged HBM and feed bandwidth with memory hierarchy (§3.4).
Define multi-die test strategy: Every die should be known good before assembly or one defect destroys package yield. Hand KGD and post-package test requirements to DFT.
Reserve scale-out interfaces: If pod deployment is planned, budget ICI pins, protocol, and bandwidth during architecture to avoid late redesign.

Document scope honestly: The v2+/pod comparison is directional TPU evolution. Interposer bump pitch, HBM generation, and exact bandwidth are generation-specific packaging details not disclosed in the v1 ISCA paper, so they remain qualitative.
source: In-Datacenter Performance Analysis of a TPU, Jouppi et al., ISCA 2017 (v1 is monolithic; v2+ 2.5D/HBM packaging and pod/ICI scaling reflect later TPU generations and standard 2.5D practice. No undisclosed v1 packaging-process values are asserted.)
